risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Study participants were individually randomised to one of two parallel groups with an allocation ratio of 1:1 using a computer-generated list. The computer-generated allocation was done by a research assistant with no clinical involvement in the trial." Comment: This describes a clearly random allocation method (computer-generated list), which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random and therefore low risk of bias.

Quote: "Study participants were individually randomised to one of two parallel groups with an allocation ratio of 1:1 using a computer-generated list. The computer-generated allocation was done by a research assistant with no clinical involvement in the trial." Comment: This describes sequence generation and who carried out the randomisation, but it does not state any concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, or identical containers) that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment. Therefore allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Pharmacists and psychiatrists were not blinded to the patients’ group allocation." Comment: The intervention was an obvious SDM-based pharmacist intervention versus usual care, and the study does not report blinding of participants. Because key personnel were unblinded and outcomes included self-reported adherence, beliefs, satisfaction, and quality of life that could plausibly be influenced by knowing allocation, performance bias is likely.

Quote: "assessments were conducted by independent raters (two trained nurses) who were blinded to patients’ group allocation." Comment: The main outcome assessments were performed by independent, blinded assessors, which lowers the risk of detection bias. Although several outcomes were self-report (e.g. MMAS, BMQ, EQ-5D, TSQM), the only clinician-rated outcome noted here (MADRS) and the other interviewer-administered assessments were collected by blinded raters; the study also states that data collection was blinded and that this was meant to limit observer bias. Therefore, knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence outcome measurement in a way that would materially bias the results.

Quote: "Of these, 19 dropped out of the study during the follow-up phase (Fig. 1) and were not included in the final analyses." Comment: Attrition was 19/239 (about 8%), which is low overall and below the usual concern threshold. The paper also states "All analyses were limited to patients who completed all assessments (complete-case analysis)," and reports no evidence of differential dropout by study arm; the only note is that dropouts were more often male and elderly, not that dropout was related to intervention or outcomes. Overall, the amount of missing outcome data appears small and unlikely to have biased the results.
